titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the James Claude Thomson and James C. Thomson Jr. Papers
Thomson (James Claude and James C. Jr.) Papers
titlestmt.author: Dionis Macy Gauvin, Martha Lund Smalley, and Abigail Kromminga

controlnote.p: This collection contains 11 published digital objects. And those objects are comprised of 3,274 digital files.

conventiondeclaration.abbr: finding aid prepared according to local divinity library descriptive practices.

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Friday December 5, 2025 at 18:02

did.unittitle: James Claude Thomson and James C. Thomson Jr. papers
did.abstract: The James Claude Thomson and James C. Thomson Papers provide a record of James Claude Thomson's career as a biochemist, professor, and nutrition expert. The collection contains correspondence from Thomson's years at the University of Nanjing from 1917-1949, which saw the Nanjing Incident, the Nanjing Massacre, and Japanese and Communist occupations. The collection also contains Claude's academic lectures, nutritional surveys of Turkey, Iran, and Pakistan conducted for the World Health Organization in the 1950s, and color slides from Iran, Afghanistan, Pakistan, China, Korea, and Japan. Addenda to the original collection documents the career of James Claude Thomson Jr., a statesman, historian, and journalist who served as a China expert in the Kennedy and Johnson administrations in the 1960s. Extensive correspondence between Thomson and his parents as well as Thomson's writings provide insight into American East Asia policy during the Cold War and Vietnam War.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: James Claude Thomson and James C. Thomson Jr. Papers (RG 24), Special Collections, Yale Divinity Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Place names were modernized in the description, with the name originally used in the collection material or in an older version of the finding aid in parenthesis: e.g. "Beijing (Peking)" or "Benin (Dahomey)".

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: James Claude Thomson (Claude) was born in New York State in 1889. He studied to be both a scientist and a pastor, graduating from Rutgers University (1912) and New Brunswick Theological Seminary (1916) with advanced degrees in chemistry and divinity. In 1917, Claude married Margaret Seabury Cook, who then became Margaret Seabury Thomson, a graduate of Smith College. They moved to Nanjing (Nanking), China with the Presbyterian Board of Foreign Missions, where Claude taught chemistry and served as dean of the University of Nanjing, and Margaret taught Bible, religion, and literature at Ginling College. Together, they had four children: Nancy Thomson (Waller), John Seabury Thomson, Sydney Thomson (Brown), and James C. Thomson Jr.

The Thomson family's tenure in Nanjing was interrupted by both routine missionary furloughs in the United States and flight from Nanjing in times of unrest and violence. During the Nanjing Incident of 1927, while the family took refuge in Shanghai, Claude oversaw the university buildings. On furlough in 1932, he completed his PhD at Columbia University. In 1937, after Japanese occupation, Claude relayed materials from Shanghai to the International Refugee Committee in Nanjing while Margaret and the children moved from Hong Kong, to Shanghai, to the East Coast of the United States, where she enrolled at Union Theological Seminary.

Following the bombing of Pearl Harbor in 1941, unable to return to China, Claude began a Master of Public Health at Johns Hopkins University. At the end of World War II in 1946, Claude traveled through North Africa and India carrying luggage filled with equipment, vaccines, and vitamins to West China Union Medical College in Chengdu (Chengtu). In his last years in China, he worked on nutritional health with orphans and individuals with leprosy before returning to Nanjing and left briefly before Communist occupation in 1949.

Humanitarian work in the field of nutrition, which Claude described as "the development of strong and healthy people who can give their very best powers to the work and thought of the world," animated the second half of Claude's career. In the 1950s, he taught nutrition as a Fulbright professor in Tehran and served as a consultant for the World Health Organization, conducting nutritional surveys of Iran, Pakistan, and Turkey.

Returning to university work and East Asia, the Thomsons taught at the International Christian University in Tokyo, Japan, from 1952 to 1955, and Yonsei University Medical College in Seoul, Korea from 1957 to 1959.
James Claude Thomson died in Swarthmore, Pennsylvania in 1974. Two years later, Margaret Seabury Thomson died in South Kent, Connecticut.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: James C. Thomson Jr. was born in 1931 in Princeton, New Jersey while the Thomson family was on furlough from missionary work in Nanjing. After spending his early childhood in China—divided between Nanjing and Hong Kong and Shanghai during times of refuge—Thomson attended the Lawrenceville School in New Jersey. In 1948, amidst the progression of Communist forces towards the Yangtze, he returned to China, briefly attending the University of Nanjing as the city filled with soldiers.

As an undergraduate at Yale College from 1949 to 1953, Thomson worked as editor in chief of the Yale Daily News . At Yale, he rubbed shoulders with prominent figures in American politics, culture, and academia, including Dean Acheson, Thornton Wilder, Chester Bowles, and fellow student journalist Gaddis Smith.

Following Yale graduation, Thomson studied at Clare College, Cambridge before beginning a doctorate at Harvard University under the mentorship of China historian John K. Fairbank in 1955. Thomson's time at Harvard was punctuated by political work: during the 1956 presidential election, Thomson worked on the Adlai Stevenson campaign (for which he coined the epithet "brinkmanship" to describe John Foster Dulles' Cold War diplomacy), and during the 1958 midterm elections, Thomson campaigned for Chester Bowles. In 1958, amidst a move from Harvard to the State Department, Thomson observed to his parents, "I am by training a historian, by practice a politician, and by instinct a journalist."

When Chester Bowles became Under Secretary of State in 1961, Thomson followed him to the State Department. By 1964, he was in his own words, "MacGeorge Bundy's Far East man," serving on the National Security Council at the White House. Though Thomson entered Washington politics with a missional drive, he left in 1966 to return to Harvard, disillusioned by attempts to change the system from the inside. His 1968 essay published in The Atlantic , "How Could Vietnam Happen? An Autopsy," provided a searing view into the inertia characterizing American actions in Vietnam. Testifying at the defense of his friend Jonathan Mirsky, an antiwar activist and historian of China, Thomson stated under oath, "if there is ever a war crimes trial, I fully expect to be a defendant."

At Harvard Thomson and his wife, the writer and teacher Diana Butler Thomson, lived at Leverett House, taught undergraduate courses, and mentored students including Richard Blumenthal and Gregory Craig. In 1972, Thomson assumed the curatorship of the Nieman Foundation at Harvard, a fellowship program for journalists. When Richard Nixon went to China in 1972, Thomson provided commentary on ABC, for which he received an Emmy Award. From 1984 until his retirement in 1997, Thomson taught journalism, history, and international relations at Boston University. While at Harvard and Boston University, he published widely in American newspapers and magazines on American East Asian policy and Chinese history and wrote two books, When China Faced West and Sentimental Imperialists: The American Experience in East Asia .
James C. Thomson Jr. died in 2002, two years after his wife Diana Butler Thomson.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This collection documents the professional and familial lives of biochemist, nutritionist, and missionary James Claude Thomson and his son, the politician, historian, and journalist James Claude Thomson Jr. Spanning two careers over nearly a century, the collection provides insight into Nanjing during the upheavals of the first half of the twentieth century, international humanitarian work in the field of nutrition in the 1950s, and Vietnam and Cold War policy in the 1960s.

Series I, Correspondence, contains detailed letters written by James Claude Thomson to his wife and family during his separations in 1938-1941 and 1949 in China, and later in the 1950s when he was on assignment in Iran and Pakistan (Series VII Addenda, includes further correspondence from these periods of separation). There are several letters from Daniel Dye, whose papers are also at the Yale Divinity Library (RG 22). Additional correspondence of Thomson from the period when he was at the University of Nanjing is available in the Archives of the United Board for Christian Higher Education in Asia (RG 11).
Series II, Papers, Reports and Speeches, primarily contains material written by Thomson on nutrition and health-related subjects.
Series III, Collected Material, includes writings on similar subjects by others, as well as miscellaneous material relating to the Thomsons' travels, and an obituary of Thomson.
Series IV, Photographs, Series V, Slides, and Series VI, Films, documents where Thomson lived and worked from the 1930s to the 1950s and contains family films from years in Nanjing.

A series of addenda added to the collection from 2003 to 2024 greatly expanded the scope of the collection, incorporating materials from the entire Thomson family, notably the correspondence and writings from James C. Thomson Jr.

In Series VII, Addenda, the first subseries, Correspondence, contains family correspondence primarily between James Claude Thomson, Margaret Seabury Thomson, and James C. Thomson Jr. and professional correspondence of James Claude Thomson. The bulk of the correspondence dating after 1957 is written by James C. Thomson Jr., describing his life in Washington. These letters provide insight into the atmosphere of both the Kennedy and Johnson administrations.

The second subseries of Series VII, Addenda, Writings, is organized by individual. Notable within the writings are the political analyses and essays by James C. Thomson Jr. and autobiographical writings from John Seabury Thomson (who served as an Asia analyst for the CIA), Nancy Thomson (Waller), and Margaret Seabury Thomson.

The third subseries, Biographical Documentation and Personal Memorabilia, is organized by individual and contains materials such as travel papers, school materials, and articles and obituaries written about Thomson family members.

The fourth subseries, Collected Material, contains materials relating to James Claude Thomson and Margaret Seabury Thomson's work in China, Japan, and Korea. Box 29 folder 1 and folder 2 contain materials relating to the Nanjing Incident and the Nanjing Massacre respectively, including missionary reflections and reports.
The fifth subseries of Series VII, Addenda, Photographs, includes portraits of James Claude Thomson Jr. throughout his career and images from a Thomson family trip to China in 1986.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These materials are by James Claude Thomson. See Series VII, Addenda, for writings and speeches by James Claude Thomson, Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This and the following folders in series II contain materials added to the collection in 2024 related to JCT's work in nutrition. For related materials on Chengtu, see also: box 2, folder 53a.

did.unittitle: Films

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Thomson family films have been digitized and are available upon request.

did.unittitle: Thomson family: four 3 1/2 inch, 16mm reels (1928-1933); twelve 2 3/4 inch 16mm reels (mostly family footage - see list of reels in Box 18, Folder 169)

Thomson family: one 5 inch 16mm reel:#1)"Jimmy and family in USA, 1931-1933"; five 7 inch 16mm reels:2)"Summer trip to USA, 1931"; 3)"Jimmy, Princeton, 1931"; 4)"DAR Picnic, 1941"; 5)"Jim, Cambridge, 1954"; 6)"Heath/ South Kent, 1956"

Thomson family / Nanking footage: three 3 1/2 inch 16mm reels: #1) "Nanking, Christmas at Mills, 1930"; 2)"Sun Yat-sen memorial, MIng tombs, etc. 1930 or 1931"; 3) "John's party, Hillcrest track meet, our house and University buildings - Nanking, 1931"

Thomson family / Nanking footage: three 4 3/4 inch 16 mm reels:# 4) "Dr. Bowen, Robin Hood - Nanking, 1929"; 5) "The trip from SG to Honolulu, Nanking and Hillcrest, 1933"; 6) "Hillcrest and Nancy's Commencement, Shanghai American School, Lotus Lake, Purple Mountain -1936"

Thomson family/ Nanking footage: four 2 3/4 inch 16mm reels: #7) "Nanking, 1933 - Emily C??"; 8) "Kuling, Nanking - swimming, camping, 1934"; 9) "Kuling, 1934"; 10)"Nanking, 1934 - Lotus Lake, Sun Yat-sen tomb, family"
Footage, mostly of Nanjing (Nanking)

Rest, Smith - 1942 ?SAS Commencement 1939?"; 10) "Unzen - 1954"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letters from JCT Jr. addressed from Chengdu (Chengtu), Chongqing (Chungking), Singapore, Malaysia, Ceylon, and India while traveling with Winthrop Knowlton. MST letters addressed form Guangzhou (Canton).

did.unittitle: To/from MST, JCT, JCT Jr., and John Seabury Thomson

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: JCT and MST letters addressed from Guangzhou, and after evacuation due to Communist takeover, Hong Kong. Letters from JCT Jr. addressed from the Red Sea, Italy, Switzerland, France, and England.

did.unittitle: To/from MST, JCT, and JCT Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: JCT Jr. in United States, at Yale University. MST and JCT in Hong Kong, before leaving on the S.S. Canton, arriving in England.

did.unittitle: To/from MST, JCT, and JCT Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: MST in the United States. Beginning February 19, letters from JCT addressed from Iran.

did.unittitle: To/from MST, JCT, and JCT Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: MST and JCT in New York.

did.unittitle: To/from MST, JCT, and JCT Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: JCT letters from Iran.

did.unittitle: To/from MST, JCT, and JCT Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: JCT letters from Afghanistan and Iran. JCT Jr. letters from France and Germany. JCT and JCT Jr. meeting in Europe.

did.unittitle: To/from MST, JCT, and JCT Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: JCT letters from Europe where he met JCT Jr.

did.unittitle: To/from MST, JCT, and JCT Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: JCT and MST in Japan at the International Christian University. JCT in Lebanon as well.

did.unittitle: To/from MST, JCT, and JCT Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: JCT in Iran and Switzerland. Letters from JCT Jr. on the Democratic National Convention in Chicago.

did.unittitle: To/from MST, JCT, JCT Jr., and Nancy Thomson Waller

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Including from MST aboard the S.S. President Cleveland.

did.unittitle: To/from MST, JCT, and JCT Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: JCT and MST at the International Christian University in Japan.

did.unittitle: To/from MST, JCT, and JCT Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: May 28, 1953 letter from JCT Jr. on dining with Thornton Wilder. Letters from MST and JCT addressed from Japan. After June, letters from JCT Jr. regarding his Europe trip.

did.unittitle: To/from MST and JCT Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: JCT Jr. letters addressed from Clare College, Cambridge and from travels across Europe.

did.unittitle: To/from MST, JCT, and JCT Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Including MST's reflections on the teachings of Emil Brunner at the International Christian University. April 14 letter from Lilliath Bates, the wife of Miner Searle Bates, a friend from the Nanjing missionary community.

did.unittitle: To/from MST, JCT, and JCT Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence from family friends addressed to MST.

did.unittitle: To/from MST and friends, JCT, and JCT Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence from family friends and students addressed to MST. JCT Jr. on summer vacation in Greece. December 12 letter from JCT describing nutritional examinations in Japan.

did.unittitle: To/from MST, JCT, JCT Jr., and Nancy Thomson Waller

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence from family friends addressed to MST. March 17 circular from MST to children and friends describing their life and work at the International Christian University. Including a letter from Mona Sun.

did.unittitle: To/from MST, JCT, JCT Jr., and others

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inlcuding correspondence from Mona Sun and other friends and students in Japan. Correspondence from JCT and MST addressed from Malaysia, India, and aboard the SS Chusan.

did.unittitle: To/from JCT, JCT Jr., and friends and students in Japan

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Including substantial correspondence from friends and students in Japan.

did.unittitle: To MST from JCT and friends

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Containing letters addressed to MST from friends and JCT in Egypt.

did.unittitle: To MST from JCT and friends

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Including from JCT in Iran.

did.unittitle: To/from JCT, JCT Jr., Nancy Thomson Waller, and friends

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letters JCT addressed from Iran and Pakistan and JCT Jr. from Harvard University. Including May 20 letter from Reiko Yokoyama containing a printed copy of his testimony for MST.

did.unittitle: To/from JCT, MST, Nancy Thomson Waller, and friends

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Extensive correspondence between JCT and MST during a period of separation while JCT worked with the UN in Ankara, Turkey.

To/from JCT and MST
To/from JCT and MST

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Including a letter from JCT Jr.

did.unittitle: To/from JCT and MST
Undated correspondence to/from MST, JCT, and JCT Jr.
From JCT Jr., JCT, and friends

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Primarily addressed from JCT Jr. at Harvard University to JCT and MST in Korea. November 12 letter from JCT Jr. on an encounter with Adlai Stevenson.

did.unittitle: From JCT Jr. and friends

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: July 8 letter describing the Chester Bowles campaign. Including a November 15 circular from MST and JCT on life in Korea.

did.unittitle: Christmas and New Year's cards from Korea
From JCT Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Initial letter from a friend. JCT Jr. again working for Chester Bowles.

did.unittitle: To/from JCT, JCT Jr., and friends

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: JCT Jr. and Diana move to Washington. MST and JCT in New York City before settling in Swarthmore for retirement.

did.unittitle: To/from JCT and JCT Jr.
From JCT and JCT Jr.
From JCT and JCT Jr.
From JCT, JCT Jr., MST, and other family

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: August 20 letter from JCT Jr. describing his work on the National Security Council.

did.unittitle: To JCT following his eye operation
To/from JCT Jr., MST, and Nancy Thomson Waller
From JCT Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: January 15 letter reflecting on Vietnam policy. Letters beginning in October are addressed from Cambridge, Massachusetts where JCT Jr. was appointed at Leverett House at Harvard.

did.unittitle: From JCT and JCT Jr.

scopecontent.p: Published in the Yale Daily News and the New York Times .

scopecontent.p: Published in The Atlantic .

did.unittitle: "How to End the War," "Recollections of a Cultural Imperialist," "Government and Press: Good news about a bad marriage"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Published in the New York Times and The Atlantic .

did.unittitle: "Kafka in the State Department," "E.O. Reischauer, Missionary"

"Journalistic Ethics: Some Probings by a Media Keeper," "China Homecoming," "China Reporting Re-Visited"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Published in Nieman Reports .

did.unittitle: "Neither Here nor There"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Reflection for the anthology My Harvard, My Yale .

did.unittitle: "Chairman Fairbank sums up China," "Refugee in New England," "Southeast Asia: the making of a foreign-policy quagmire," "Pearl Buck: a literary diplomat in China," "The Day Pearl Buck and the Nobel Committee Shocked the World"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Published in the Boston Sunday Globe , the New England Journal of Public Policy , the Hartford Courant , and the Washington Post .

did.unittitle: "Statement for Metcalf Committee," "Article for GBH"
"Jilted Again: The U.S. Media's Courtship with Democracy in China"
"One Historian's Lament," memorial to John Fairbank, address at the Pearl S. Buck Symposium

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Including a draft of an op ed on the Gulf War.

did.unittitle: "Pearl S. Buck and the American Quest for China," "Pearl S. Buck at One Hundred: An East-West Mystery," "The Rogue Elephant and Us," "The Actress and the Novelist"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Articles celebrating the centennial of Pearl S. Buck's life published in the Los Angeles Times Book Review and The New York Times Book Review .

did.unittitle: "Introduction of McGeorge Bundy," "The lesson of Tiananmen: Time is on democracy's side," "On Heroes, Feet of Clay and J.W. Fulbright," "A Memory of McGeorge Bundy"

scopecontent.p: Published in The Boston Sunday Globe and the New York Times .

Regarding the Nanjing Incident

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Containing "A Statement to Chinese Friends," "Two Years After," Nanjing Station Report, 1928 - 1929, and an undated and unsigned personal account of the incident.

did.unittitle: Regarding the Nanjing Massacre

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Including "Note on Aftermath of Registration at the University" by M. Searle Bates, 1937, transcript of article from Hein Shun Pao "Disastrous Fire at the Soviet Embassy Nanking," 1938, transcript of article from Sin Shun Pao "Japanese Troops Gently Soothe the Refugees," 1938, "Notes on the Immediate Situation as Affecting the University of Nanking" with cover letter to American Embassy, by M. Searle Bates, 1938, letter to "Dear Friends" from M. Searle Bates, 1938, articles from New York Times "At the Rape of Nanking: A Nazi Who Saved Lives," 1996.

did.unittitle: "Sketches of Nanking: Papers of the Literary Department of the Nanking Woman's Club"
Regarding Ginling College and West China Union University
Maps
Missionary circulars and writings

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Containing "To the missionaries of the China dispersion" by E.E. Walline 1954, a letter from Tehran, "Nanking Neighbors" by Lilian C. Williams, and "Nanking" by Joyce Caldwell.

did.unittitle: Related to work at International Christian University

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Including letters and papers from Margaret Thomson's Bible class, a 1958 circular from JCT addressed to ICU students, and "The Christian University and its Importance for Japan" by Emil Brunner.

Miscellaneous clippings and booklets
